narrative: As a Black male applicant with an upper-middle-class family background and plans to attend law school full-time, your profile stood out in several ways, including your undergraduate GPA of 3.20—which is right around the typical 3.21 average for students in our dataset—and an LSAT score of 31, which is below the average of about 37. However, the admissions model, trained on past students' data to predict first-time bar exam success, estimated a 72.5% chance that you would fail the bar exam, tipping the decision toward rejecting your application to help the school maintain strong bar passage rates. The most influential factor pushing toward this failure prediction was your Year 3 school decile ranking of 1, meaning your grades placed you in the bottom tenth of your peers that year, far below the average decile of around 5.5. Next in importance was your Year 1 decile also at 1, again well below the typical 5.7 average, signaling early academic struggles that the model sees as a strong warning sign for bar exam performance. While your undergraduate GPA provided some counterbalance by pulling the prediction slightly toward success—being so close to the norm—it wasn't enough to offset those lower high school rankings. Compared to students who typically pass the bar on their first try, your position in the lowest deciles for both early and later years is uncommon and carried the heaviest weight in the model's eyes, unlike many applicants who rank in the middle or higher. This isn't a judgment on your potential or background, but a data-driven assessment based on patterns from thousands of similar students. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score closer to or above average, as that could meaningfully shift future predictions. Strengthening your recent academic record through additional coursework or a post-baccalaureate program might also help elevate those decile rankings in a reapplication. Reaching out to admissions advisors for targeted feedback or exploring bridge programs designed for underrepresented students like yourself could open doors at schools with more holistic reviews. With focused improvements, especially in academics and test scores, you're well-positioned to reapply stronger next cycle. Your full-time commitment is a plus, aligning with the vast majority of successful bar takers. Keep pushing—many students overcome early hurdles to excel in law.
